conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility properly, I need to ask you some questions.

First, let me read the official guidance to ensure I give you accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for waiting. Now let me help you determine your eligibility for Child Benefit.

To start, do you have any children that you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I have one child - Alex, who's 8 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. 

Do you live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, thank you. 

Do you live with Alex, or do they live with someone else?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex doesn't live with me - they live with someone else.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I understand. Since Alex doesn't live with you, I need to ask: do you contribute financially towards Alex's upkeep? This could include things like food, clothes, pocket money, or contributing to their accommodation.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do help out a bit - I contribute towards things like clothes and bits and pieces for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. To determine your eligibility, I need to understand the amount you're contributing.

Are you contributing at least the same amount as the Child Benefit payment (or the equivalent value) towards Alex's upkeep? For reference, the Child Benefit rate for one child is currently around £25 per week (approximately £102-108 per month).

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: To be honest, it's not that much - I contribute about £10 a week towards Alex's needs.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for being honest about that. I have one more important question: Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Let me assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Based on the official guidance, to qualify for Child Benefit when a child doesn't live with you, you must be contributing at least the same amount as the Child Benefit payment (or the equivalent in kind) towards looking after them. This includes things like food, clothes, or pocket money.

The Child Benefit rate for one child is approximately £25 per week. Since you're contributing about £10 per week towards Alex's needs, this falls short of the required amount to qualify for Child Benefit when the child lives with someone else.

However, there is an important consideration: if Alex has only recently gone to live with someone else, you could still receive Child Benefit for up to 8 weeks after they moved, even if nobody else is claiming. After that 8-week period, you would need to be contributing at least the Child Benefit amount to continue receiving it.

Could you tell me when Alex went to live with the other person? Was it within the last 8 weeks, or has it been longer than that?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: It's been quite a while now - definitely more than 8 weeks ago.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying that. I now have all the information I need to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on my assessment, I'm sorry to inform you that **you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**.

Here's why:

While Alex meets the age requirement (being 8 years old and under 16), and you live in the UK, the key issue is that Alex doesn't live with you. According to the Child Benefit rules, when a child lives with someone else, you can still qualify if you're contributing at least the same amount as the Child Benefit payment towards their upkeep.

The current Child Benefit rate for one child is approximately £25 per week. You're contributing about £10 per week, which unfortunately falls short of this requirement.

Additionally, the 8-week grace period (which allows you to continue receiving Child Benefit after a child goes to live elsewhere, even without sufficient contributions) has passed since it's been more than 8 weeks.

**What you could consider:**
- If you were able to increase your contributions to at least £25 per week (or equivalent value in clothes, food, pocket money, etc.), you could potentially become eligible
- The person Alex lives with may be eligible to claim Child Benefit if they are responsible for Alex's day-to-day care

If you have any questions about this decision or your circumstances change, you can contact the Child Benefit Office for further guidance.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£10.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£10.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
circumstances: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£10.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old, which meets the age requirement (under 16). The claimant lives in the UK, which satisfies the residence requirement. However, Alex does not live with the claimant - they live with someone else. According to the eligibility rules, when a child doesn't live with you, you must be contributing at least the same amount as the Child Benefit payment (or equivalent in kind) towards looking after them to qualify. The claimant contributes approximately £10 per week, but the Child Benefit rate for one child is approximately £25 per week. Since the contribution (£10/week) is less than the Child Benefit amount (£25/week), the claimant does not meet the financial contribution threshold. Additionally, the 8-week grace period after a child goes to live with someone else has passed (it's been more than 8 weeks). Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (aged 8), who lives with someone else. While the claimant makes some financial contributions (£10/week), this falls below the required threshold of at least the Child Benefit amount (approximately £25/week) needed to qualify when a child doesn't live with you. The 8-week grace period has also expired. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit.

The claimant contributes approximately £10 per week, but the Child Benefit rate for one child is approximately £25 per week. Since the contribution (£10/week) is less than the Child Benefit amount (£25/week), the claimant does not meet the financial contribution threshold. Additionally, the 8-week grace period after a child goes to live with someone else has passed (it's been more than 8 weeks). Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

While the claimant makes some financial contributions (£10/week), this falls below the required threshold of at least the Child Benefit amount (approximately £25/week) needed to qualify when a child doesn't live with you. The 8-week grace period has also expired. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit.
